Glossary About
Nineteenth Century

Hermann Lotze

1817 – 1881

A bridge from idealism to empirical psychology: soul, value, and the unity of mental life.

Rudolf Hermann Lotze’s philosophy of mind is best reconstructed as a non-reductive, psychophysical, value-oriented theory of the soul as a unitary subject of consciousness. It is not Cartesian dualism in its simple textbook form, because the soul is not treated as an isolated mental substance sealed off from nature. It is not materialism, because mental life cannot be derived from neural or bodily processes. It is not vitalism, because Lotze rejects occult “vital forces” as pseudo-explanations. It is not panpsychism in any simple doctrinal sense, because although Lotze allows that soul-like inwardness may extend more widely than human and animal consciousness, he repeatedly treats such extension as scientifically unfruitful or merely possible. And it is not psychologism, because psychology does not ground logic, truth, or validity.

The resulting position is more subtle: the body supplies the conditions, stimuli, media, and limits of mental life; the soul actively produces sensations, relations, feelings, attention, self-consciousness, and volitions according to its own nature; and both are situated within a wider metaphysical order in which mechanism and teleology are compatible. Lotze is therefore one of the major nineteenth-century mediators between German idealism, medical physiology, early scientific psychology, and later philosophy of mind.

1. Context, sources, and terminology

Lotze lived from 1817 to 1881 and was trained in both medicine and philosophy. He received doctorates in both fields at Leipzig, taught in medicine and philosophy, succeeded Herbart at Göttingen, and later held a chair in Berlin shortly before his death. This dual formation matters greatly: his philosophy of mind is not an armchair metaphysics placed over against physiology, but an attempt to interpret the new physiology of the nervous system without letting it collapse into materialism.

Modern readers should treat Lotze’s vocabulary carefully. He often speaks of Seele, translated as “soul,” but in his context the term covers much of what today would be divided among “mind,” “subjectivity,” “consciousness,” “personhood,” and “psychical life.” Berrios notes that Lotze’s concept of Seele is precisely one of the reasons he could appear too metaphysical to materialists and too biological to spiritualists. George Santayana’s warning about Lotze is useful here: old metaphysical words often carry new, more disciplined meanings in his work. The Stanford Encyclopedia likewise presents Lotze as a transitional figure between German idealism and the later scientific, academic ethos of philosophy.

The compact primary text most directly relevant here is the posthumously published Outlines of Psychology, based on Lotze’s lectures and translated into English in 1886. Its editor emphasizes that Lotze was “a born psychologist” and that much of his work concerned “the human soul.” The report below also draws on the broader system represented by his Medicinische Psychologie, Microcosmus, Logic, and Metaphysics, but the Outlines provides an especially clear route into his psychology.

2. The basic program: mechanism without materialism, teleology without vitalism

Lotze’s central problem is how to preserve the legitimacy of natural-scientific explanation while denying that such explanation exhausts the meaning of mental life. He thought physiology and mechanism were indispensable, but he also thought that purely mechanical explanation cannot tell us why the world matters, why experience has value, or why consciousness has its distinctive qualitative and unified character. Berrios summarizes this double aim well: Lotze wanted physiology’s mechanisms to be used in explaining behavior, but only while respecting meaning and context; at the same time, he wanted to eliminate mysterious vital-force explanations.

This is the key to what may be called Lotze’s teleomechanism. Mechanism explains the regular connections of phenomena; teleology interprets the value, significance, and purposive ordering of the whole. He does not use teleology as a substitute for causal explanation. Instead, he treats teleology as a higher-level interpretation of why a mechanistic order is meaningful. The Stanford Encyclopedia emphasizes that Lotze opposed vitalistic explanations while holding that mechanism and teleology are compatible; it even says one can be, on Lotze’s terms, both a physiologist of the brain and nervous system and a metaphysician of the soul.

This makes Lotze historically important. He tries to block two reductions at once. Against crude materialism, he insists that mind cannot be explained away as matter in motion. Against speculative idealism or spiritualism, he insists that mind must be studied in connection with the nervous system, bodily expression, pathology, and empirical psychology. His psychology is therefore neither “science alone” nor “metaphysics alone,” but a philosophical interpretation of psychophysical facts.

3. The subject matter of psychology: sensations, ideas, feelings, will

Lotze defines the facts of psychology broadly. The Outlines begins by grouping “sensations, ideas, feelings, acts of will” as the facts making up the life of the soul. He distinguishes three tasks: a descriptive or empirical psychology that records mental phenomena; an explanatory or mechanical psychology that seeks their conditions and lawful combinations; and an ideal or speculative psychology that asks about their ultimate significance.

That division already shows how far Lotze is from a merely intellectualist philosophy of mind. Mind is not just cognition. A full account must include sensation, representation, association, attention, spatial perception, feeling, self-consciousness, bodily action, will, pathology, and the metaphysical status of the soul. In this respect Lotze differs from any view that identifies the mind primarily with thought or judgment. The Stanford Encyclopedia notes the same point: for Lotze, the human soul is equally the source of cognition, emotion, and volition, and he criticizes the Cartesian tendency to make cognition the essence of mentality.

This broader conception is crucial for extracting a philosophy of mind from Lotze. The mind is not simply a container of representations. It is a living, feeling, willing, relating subject, embodied in a nervous organism and oriented toward value.

4. Sensation and the psychophysical gap

Lotze’s treatment of sensation is one of the most philosophically modern parts of his psychology. He begins from physiology: external stimuli are motions or physical processes, and nerve excitations propagate toward the brain. But he sharply denies that the physical description of such events contains, resembles, or logically explains the resulting sensation.

His argument is not that physiology is false. It is that physiology changes the question. A nerve process may be necessary for sensation, but a nerve is not itself a subject of sensation. Lotze rejects the idea that we should speak of “nerve sensations” as though a nerve fiber itself had a psychical life. Our own sensation is the datum; the supposed sensation of a nerve is an empty conjecture.

He then makes the stronger philosophical point: the qualitative content of sensation and the activity of being conscious of it are not comparable with physical motion. No description of motion makes it self-evident why there should be red, pain, tone, pressure, warmth, or any other sensory quality. The physical and psychical series are lawfully connected, but the necessity connecting them is not transparent to us.

In contemporary terms, Lotze anticipates something like the explanatory gap between physical process and phenomenal consciousness. He does not formulate this as a modern anti-physicalist argument about “qualia,” but his point is structurally similar: a neural process may correlate with an experience, but the intrinsic character of experience is not deducible from the physical description alone.

The correct Lotzean formula is therefore:

physical stimulus → nervous process → psychical response of the soul,
but not
physical process = sensation.

This is why his view is not reductive materialism. It is also why it is not mere mystery-mongering. He accepts lawful correlation, but denies conceptual reduction.

5. The mind-body relation: reciprocal action without reduction

Lotze’s mind-body theory is often described as interactionist, but his interactionism is not the simple Cartesian image of two independently complete substances pushing each other across an ontological gap. He argues that all causation is obscure if we imagine it as the transfer of a ready-made state from one thing into another. When one thing acts on another, the first thing’s state is an occasion for the second thing to produce a new state from its own nature. The effect need not resemble the cause.

This gives him a powerful reply to the standard objection that an immaterial soul cannot interact with a material body. Lotze’s answer is that material-material causation is not ultimately more intelligible than mind-body causation. In both cases, what we actually know is a lawful order of conditional occurrence. If a bodily process can occasion another bodily process whose inner production we do not fully comprehend, then a bodily process can also occasion a psychical process, provided the soul is the sort of being whose nature responds in that way.

Lotze also denies that a special “bond” must be inserted between body and soul. Every reciprocal action is itself a bond. This is a significant move: he refuses to solve the mind-body problem by adding a third entity, a mediator, a vital force, or an occult bridge. The relation is primitive only in the sense in which all real causation is ultimately primitive.

His position can be reconstructed as psychophysical interaction under a unified world-order. The body does not manufacture consciousness as a factory manufactures a product. Rather, bodily processes stimulate, condition, inhibit, and mediate the soul’s activities. Conversely, the soul’s willing, attending, feeling, and representing can become conditions for bodily expression and movement.

6. Why materialism fails: unity of consciousness

Lotze’s deepest anti-materialist argument is not merely that sensation is qualitatively different from motion. It is that consciousness is unified. A plurality of physical elements, or even a plurality of tiny psychical elements distributed through the body, cannot by itself explain the unity of a single conscious field.

He considers and rejects a kind of psychicalized materialism: the view that every bodily element might have its own psychical force. This does not solve the problem; it multiplies psychical points without explaining the unity of consciousness. Lotze says such a theory “goes to wreck” on the unity of consciousness, because the conscious subject must be an indivisible point of collection or synthesis.

The soul, then, is not inferred merely because we perceive ourselves as unified. Lotze’s stronger claim is that anything’s appearing to us at all presupposes a unified subject to which it appears. The Stanford Encyclopedia presents this as a transcendental argument: the unity of the soul is required not because we introspect unity as an object, but because appearing itself requires a single subject of appearance.

This gives Lotze a distinctive place between Kant and metaphysical psychology. Like Kant, he sees unity as a condition of experience. Unlike Kant, he is more willing to speak of the soul as a real unitary subject. But he also avoids crude substance metaphysics: the soul is not a hidden lump behind consciousness; its nature is known through what it does.

7. The soul as real, indivisible, immaterial, and embodied

Lotze’s soul is an indivisible subject of consciousness, distinct from the body’s multiplicity. In the Outlines, he says there is one indivisible subject of consciousness, separate from the body, while the body is an ordered multiplicity that may be kindred to the soul but is not identical with it.

Yet this soul is not a ghost floating in empty abstraction. It is embodied. Lotze allows that an immaterial being has no extension, but he also argues that it can have a position, understood not geometrically as a spread-out body but functionally as the point or system of points through which influences are received and actions mediated.

He then complicates the picture further. The soul need not be present at only one mathematical point. It can have several special positions without being extended. Its “seat” is not first fixed spatially and then related to the body; rather, its systematic relations of reciprocal action determine its spatial position within the bodily order.

Physiologically, Lotze connects the soul most directly with the central nervous organs. He denies the naive belief that the soul is equally and immediately present throughout the body. The rest of the body is connected with the soul through nervous mediation. At the same time, if one could inspect the brain microscopically, one would not see little psychical products being generated by matter. One would see physical processes that serve as stimuli upon which the soul, according to its own nature, exercises its capacities.

This is a sophisticated nineteenth-century form of embodied dual-aspect or interactionist thought. The soul is immaterial in the sense that it is not an extended body; it is embodied in the sense that its actual life is mediated through bodily organization.

8. The origin and immortality of the soul

Lotze is cautious about immortality. He does not think that calling the soul a substance proves that it is eternal. A thing is not immortal simply because we can attach the word “substance” to it. The soul is a real being because it is conscious of itself as a unitary subject, but that does not prove that its conscious capacity must have existed from eternity or must continue forever.

His position is subtle: the soul may originate “in and with” the body, but not “of and through” the body. That is, its appearance may coincide with the formation of an organic germ, but its reality is not mechanically produced by bodily matter. It is grounded in the deeper metaphysical order of the world.

On immortality, Lotze offers a value-based rather than demonstrative doctrine. The soul endures if it has an unalterable value within the coherent order of the world; but human beings cannot theoretically know that this is so. This is important: Lotze’s doctrine of the soul is not a straightforward apologetic proof of personal immortality. It is a metaphysical possibility governed by value, not a psychological theorem.

9. Incorporeal minds, God, and the wider metaphysical order

Lotze’s finite soul is immaterial but normally embodied. He does not, in psychology, give a detailed positive account of free-floating incorporeal minds. His clearest commitments are these: finite souls are real unitary subjects; they are not reducible to bodies; they are actualized in bodily life; and their interaction with bodies is possible only within the larger unity of reality.

At the metaphysical level, Lotze tends toward a form of objective idealist or personalist monism. Interaction among finite beings is ultimately possible because they are not absolutely independent atoms; they are dependent modifications or members of one truly existent Being. In the Outlines, he says reciprocal action is possible only if the interacting elements are dependent modifications of one Being that grounds both existence and action.

The Stanford Encyclopedia connects this with Lotze’s view that personality is an ethical concept and, in the highest sense, ultimate reality. God is not merely a first cause but the supreme Good, and the human soul must be understood through cognition, emotion, and volition together.

Thus, Lotze’s philosophy of mind is inseparable from his metaphysics of value. The soul is not merely a psychological machine. It is a finite center of experience, feeling, striving, and valuation within a purposive cosmic order.

10. Panpsychism, plant souls, animal souls

Lotze sometimes seems sympathetic to panpsychism, but his position is deliberately guarded. He allows that “endowing things with a soul” may extend further than the strict explanatory requirement. He even says the thought that all things have souls may have good grounds. But he immediately adds that such a thought has so far been unfruitful for explaining individual phenomena. He treats plant souls in the same cautious way: possible, perhaps attractive, but difficult to make scientifically useful.

The animal kingdom is different. Animals provide an ascending series of spiritual life. Lotze defines “soul” broadly as a title for beings that experience their own inner states and reactions to stimuli in the form of ideas, feelings, and acts of will. But he denies that all animal souls are originally the same kind of being merely equipped with different faculties. The inner nature of souls may differ radically across forms of life.

The Stanford Encyclopedia gives the same caution at the systematic level: Lotze’s statements on panpsychism are equivocal. Panpsychism may be possible and aesthetically attractive, but Lotze does not present it as an actual or probable scientific doctrine; science does not need it for its explanations.

So the best reconstruction is this: Lotze is not a dogmatic panpsychist. He is a guarded, metaphysically permissive anti-reductionist who thinks soul-like inwardness may extend beyond humans, but who reserves scientific seriousness for cases where inner life is explanatorily required.

11. Ideas, association, and the limits of mental mechanics

Lotze accepts association. Ideas become associated when they occur simultaneously or in immediate succession. But he rejects the view that the whole of mental life can be explained by a mechanics of ideas. The course of thought depends not merely on formal association but on mood, common feeling, bodily life, personal interest, and the whole condition of the subject. For that reason, he denies that we can produce a precise mechanical theory of every actual train of thought.

His critique of associationism becomes sharper when he turns to relations. The mind does not merely undergo ideas; it compares, relates, distinguishes, unifies, and judges. A relation between ideas is not the same thing as the mind’s representation of that relation. For comparison to occur, ideas must be held apart and brought together by an activity of the subject. Higher-order representations such as similarity, contrast, and relation are not mechanically generated by the mere collision of simpler ideas. They are new spiritual activities elicited from the whole subject.

This is one of Lotze’s major contributions to philosophy of mind. He is neither a passive empiricist nor a pure rationalist. Experience supplies material, but the mind actively transforms that material into relations, judgments, and meanings.

12. Attention and the degrees of consciousness

Lotze’s account of attention also resists reduction to mental mechanics. He rejects the Herbartian idea that attention is merely the rise of an idea above others by its own strength. Attention is not simply brightness in the field of consciousness. It is an activity of relating, comparing, and assigning significance.

He distinguishes degrees of consciousness in a philosophically important way. An idea may be present; it may also be connected with other ideas; and it may further be apprehended as significant for the person’s life. Attention therefore involves not only cognitive selection but personal relevance. This connects cognition with value and feeling.

In modern terms, Lotze’s attention is not just a spotlight model. It is closer to an evaluative-organizational function of the subject: attention structures the mental field according to relevance, relation, and personal importance.

13. Space perception and local signs

Lotze’s theory of spatial perception is one of his most historically influential psychological doctrines. He asks how non-spatial sensations can give rise to the representation of spatial order. A retinal image is not enough. Its mere existence on the retina does not explain how the mind represents points as left, right, above, below, near, or far.

His key insight is that the mental representation of spatial order is not itself spatial in the same way as its object. The idea of a left point is not literally located to the left of the idea of a right point. The presentation is not what it presents. Mental contents may represent spatial relations without themselves being laid out in physical space.

To explain how spatial order is constructed, Lotze introduces local signs: qualitative marks or accessory impressions associated with stimulation at different bodily or retinal locations. The mind uses these signs to order sensations spatially. The signs themselves are not spatial extension; they are intensive qualitative differences that the soul transforms into spatial representation.

This doctrine influenced later theories of perception. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy notes that Helmholtz developed Lotze’s sign-theory idea into the view that sense organs do not provide isomorphic pictures of the outer world but signs that perception transforms into pictures. Wundt also took Lotze’s local signs seriously, while criticizing him for leaving too much to the mind’s organizing activity.

Philosophically, the local-sign theory supports Lotze’s larger view: the mind is not a passive receiver of spatial form; it actively reconstructs spatiality from sensory conditions.

14. Feeling, value, and the affective structure of mind

Feeling is not secondary in Lotze. It is central. Feelings of pleasure and pain differ from sensations, though they are closely connected with them. A sensation presents a content; a feeling expresses the relation of that content to the welfare, harmony, or disturbance of the subject. Lotze even suggests that no presentation is wholly indifferent.

He treats feelings as tokens of agreement or disagreement between excitations and the soul’s enduring well-being. This is not usually a conscious judgment. It is a result of the subject’s whole nature responding to what affects it.

Lotze then extends this to ethical and aesthetic life. Moral approbation and disapprobation are not theoretical judgments of truth and falsity; they are ways in which value is disclosed in feeling. This is one of the deepest links between his psychology and his value theory. Feeling is not merely a subjective coloring added to cognition; it is a mode in which value becomes manifest to the subject.

This also explains why Lotze cannot be comfortably classified as an intellectualist. Thought, feeling, and will are integrated. The soul is a center of valuation as much as cognition.

15. Self-consciousness: “mine” before “I”

Lotze’s theory of self-consciousness is one of his most original contributions. He denies that the self can be explained merely as an object represented by a subject. The formula “the ego is both subject and object” is insufficient, because it fails to explain how a state is recognized as mine rather than merely represented.

His answer is affective. My own states announce themselves with immediate feeling: pleasure, pain, interest, concern, or felt involvement. Merely represented states of others do not have this same immediate character. Bare cognition cannot generate the distinction between mine and not-mine; the possessive “mine” is prior to the reflective concept of the “I.”

This is why Lotze says self-consciousness is a later interpretation by thought of an inner experience originally possible only as feeling. The self is first lived and felt, then conceptually identified.

Recent scholarship has rediscovered this feature. Mark Textor’s recent work explicitly presents Lotze as opposing Kantian and Fichtean treatments of self-consciousness as purely epistemic, arguing instead that self-consciousness constitutively involves feeling.

The significance for philosophy of mind is considerable. Lotze anticipates later phenomenological and affective theories of selfhood: the self is not first an object of inner perception, nor merely the logical subject of judgment, but a structure of felt mineness.

16. Will, action, and embodiment

Lotze’s account of action is equally anti-reductive and embodied. We do not know how to move our muscles by representing the muscles or nerves involved. The soul produces an inner state—wishing, desiring, willing—and the order of nature connects that state with bodily movement.

He distinguishes reflex movements, expressive or mimetic movements, habitual skilled movements, and fully voluntary action. Reflex motions may occur without consciousness and without the soul’s cooperation. Expressive actions can arise from psychical states without explicit volition. Skilled actions such as writing or playing an instrument may proceed through learned associations between ideas and movements.

The will itself “can do no more than will.” It does not micromanage motor execution. Bodily accomplishment depends on the natural order linking states of the soul to motor processes. Voluntary action is distinguished by the adoption or endorsement of an inner state by the will; involuntary action may involve similar psychophysical mechanisms but lacks that personal endorsement.

In the later chapter on the realm of souls, Lotze sharpens the point: genuine willing occurs when motives and their values have been compared with full consciousness and a decision is made. The phrase “I will” does not merely predict future behavior; it expresses personal approval or adoption of a decision proceeding from the ego.

This view is compatible with lawful explanation but resists mechanical reduction. Lotze even says psychology should not be misused to decide the metaphysical question of freedom from supposed introspective evidence.

17. Pathology, memory, localization, and the limits of cerebral explanation

Lotze’s philosophy of mind is not indifferent to pathology. He discusses unconsciousness, sleep, hypnosis, memory loss, temperament, aphasia, and the localization of functions. The general pattern is consistent: bodily disturbances can affect mental life, but that does not prove that bodily organs produce the higher acts of consciousness as their sufficient cause.

He considers possible explanations of bodily dependence: an organ might be a productive cause, a medium of stimuli, or an inhibiting condition. He rejects the first interpretation when it claims that the organ simply produces the psychical function. The second and third interpretations fit his broader view: the body mediates, conditions, and limits the soul’s activity.

On memory, he is cautious. He does not think a corporeal basis is logically required by the concept of memory, and he rejects crude physical analogies. But he acknowledges that pathological observations show some bodily basis for memory must exist. Selective loss of names, words, or categories shows dependence on brain processes, though the details remain obscure.

His discussion of aphasia is especially revealing. He acknowledges that the speech organ is among the few functions localized with some certainty in the brain; damage can destroy the capacity to combine sound-images of words with speech movements. But for higher spiritual capacities, especially judgment of relations, he denies that we know how to identify a bodily organ that would explain the essential act of judgment itself.

So Lotze’s neuropsychology is recognizably nineteenth-century and partly outdated, but his conceptual distinction remains important: the bodily basis of a capacity is not identical with the act’s logical or phenomenological essence.

18. Anti-psychologism: psychology does not ground truth

Lotze’s philosophy of mind must be connected with his logic. He rejects psychologism, the view that logical validity can be reduced to psychological processes. Psychology can study how thoughts arise, how concepts are formed in the mind, and how judgments occur as mental events. But it cannot thereby explain the validity of logical relations or truths. The Stanford Encyclopedia notes that, for Lotze, psychology comes “last, not first,” and that he distinguishes the act of thought from its content in a way that anticipates later anti-psychologistic traditions.

This is crucial. Lotze’s mind is active in judging, but the validity of a judgment is not made valid by the mental act. There is a difference between the psychological genesis of a thought and the objective validity of what is thought. This distinction influenced later neo-Kantianism, Fregean logic, phenomenology, and analytic philosophy. The Internet Encyclopedia notes, for example, that Frege followed Lotze on context-related aspects of judgment and that Windelband developed truth-value in the wake of Lotzean concerns.

Thus Lotze contributes to philosophy of mind not only by discussing consciousness but also by limiting psychology’s authority. Mind is the place where thinking occurs; it is not the source of logical validity.

19. Extracted systematic philosophy of mind

A comprehensive Lotzean philosophy of mind can be stated in the following theses.

First, the mind is a soul, where “soul” means a real, indivisible subject of experience, feeling, thought, and will. It is not a bundle of representations, not a brain process, and not a mere logical abstraction.

Second, consciousness presupposes unity. A multiplicity of bodily or psychical elements cannot by aggregation produce the unity required for anything to appear to a subject.

Third, mental qualities are irreducible to physical motions. Stimuli and neural processes are lawfully connected with sensations, but the qualitative character of sensation is not deducible from mechanics.

Fourth, the body is indispensable but not sufficient. The body mediates stimuli, provides organs of perception and action, enables memory and skill, and imposes pathological limits. But bodily processes do not by themselves generate the essential acts of consciousness, judgment, feeling, or willing.

Fifth, mind-body causation is reciprocal and lawful. Bodily states occasion psychical responses; psychical states can occasion bodily movements. This is possible because causation is not the transfer of similar stuff but the occasion for a being to respond according to its nature.

Sixth, the mind is active. It does not passively receive sensations and associations. It relates, compares, attends, synthesizes, spatializes, judges, values, and wills.

Seventh, spatial perception is constructed. Sensory data are not already spatial in the required sense. Local signs provide qualitative cues that the soul transforms into spatial representation.

Eighth, feeling is cognitively and metaphysically central. Feeling discloses the relation of contents to the subject’s welfare and to value. It also grounds the lived mineness of self-consciousness.

Ninth, selfhood begins affectively. The self is not first known by a detached act of inner observation. The “mine” of experience is felt before the “I” is conceptually articulated.

Tenth, will is personal endorsement. Volition is not the mechanical strongest-idea theory. It involves comparison of motives, valuation, and the personal adoption of a decision.

Eleventh, psychology is not the foundation of logic. Psychological processes explain how thoughts occur, not why judgments are valid.

Twelfth, mind belongs to a value-laden metaphysical order. The soul is intelligible only within a world in which mechanism is real but subordinate to meaning, value, personality, and the Good.

20. Historical position in the development of philosophy of mind

Lotze stands at a crossroads. He inherits from Leibniz and Herbart the idea that mental unity cannot be explained by material aggregation, but he rejects a purely formal or mechanical “psychical mechanics” of ideas. He inherits from Kant the importance of the unity of consciousness and the active contribution of the mind, but he gives these themes a more psychological and physiological setting. He inherits from German idealism the conviction that mind and value are central to reality, but he rejects speculative excess and insists on the legitimacy of empirical science.

Against Cartesianism, Lotze denies that the mind is essentially only thinking. The soul is also feeling and willing, and its life is bodily mediated. Against materialism, he denies that neural mechanics can explain sensation, unity, selfhood, value, or judgment. Against vitalism, he denies that unexplained life-forces should replace mechanism. Against associationism, he denies that higher mental functions can be built up mechanically from simple ideas. Against psychologism, he denies that logic and validity are reducible to mental events.

His historical influence was unusually wide. The Stanford Encyclopedia lists his impact on Windelband, Frege, Green, Bradley, Bosanquet, Royce, William James, Santayana, and early Moore and Russell. The Internet Encyclopedia similarly describes him as a key figure for neo-Kantians, Brentano’s school, British idealism, James’s pragmatism, Husserlian phenomenology, Dilthey, Frege, and early Cambridge analytic philosophy. A recent Routledge reassessment likewise frames Lotze as a leading late-nineteenth-century figure whose work helped shape analytic philosophy, phenomenology, and pragmatism.

In the narrower history of philosophy of mind and psychology, his importance lies especially in five areas: the critique of materialism, the theory of psychophysical incomparability, the unity of consciousness argument, the local-sign theory of spatial perception, and the affective theory of self-consciousness. His work helped connect German metaphysics with physiological psychology and later phenomenological, pragmatist, and analytic concerns.

21. Relation to modern philosophy of mind

Lotze is not a contemporary physicalist, functionalist, dualist, or panpsychist in any straightforward sense. But his thought anticipates several modern debates.

His claim that no physical motion makes sensation intelligible anticipates the explanatory gap and the problem of phenomenal consciousness. His unity argument anticipates debates over whether consciousness can be explained by aggregation. His local-sign theory anticipates constructivist and inferential accounts of perception. His affective account of self-consciousness anticipates phenomenological discussions of mineness, bodily self-awareness, and depersonalization. His anti-psychologism anticipates the separation between cognitive psychology and logic or semantics.

At the same time, Lotze resists some modern categories. He is not a physicalist, because he denies reductive explanation of consciousness by the body. He is not a standard substance dualist, because his soul is functionally embodied and grounded in a monistic metaphysical order. He is not a neutral monist in the later Russellian sense, because value, personality, and the Good are metaphysically central. He is not a straightforward panpsychist, because he treats universal soul-attribution as possible but scientifically unproductive. And he is not a computationalist, because cognition for him is not symbol manipulation alone but an activity of a feeling, willing, valuing subject.

22. Critical assessment

Lotze’s greatest strength is that he refuses a false choice between science and mind. He treats physiology seriously while exposing the limits of physiological reduction. He sees that the body conditions consciousness, but he does not infer that consciousness is nothing but body. He also sees that mental life includes value, feeling, attention, selfhood, and will, not merely representation.

His theory of self-consciousness is especially strong. The idea that mineness is affective before it is reflective remains philosophically fertile. Many contemporary theories still struggle to explain why a mental state is experienced as mine rather than merely occurring. Lotze’s answer, that felt concern precedes explicit self-conception, is still worth taking seriously.

His main weakness is that his metaphysical solution to mind-body interaction depends on a broad theory of reality as a unified order grounded in one Being. This may be powerful within his system, but it will not satisfy philosophers who want an explanation compatible with metaphysical naturalism. His reply that all causation is ultimately obscure also weakens the force of objections to interactionism, but it does not give a fully positive mechanism.

His doctrine of the soul also faces pressure from later neuroscience, especially split-brain research, dissociation, modularity, and disorders of self-representation. Lotze can accommodate pathology as disturbance of bodily mediation, but his strong indivisibility thesis would need refinement to engage modern neuropsychology.

Even so, Lotze’s project remains philosophically important because it articulates a position still underexplored today: a rigorous, medically informed, anti-reductionist philosophy of mind centered on unity, embodiment, affective selfhood, and value.

23. Final synthesis

Lotze’s comprehensive philosophy of mind can be summarized as follows.

The mind is a real soul: an indivisible, self-active subject of sensations, ideas, feelings, and will. It is immaterial in the sense that it is not an extended bodily object, but it is not detached from embodiment. The body provides the organized nervous conditions through which the soul receives stimuli, constructs spatial perception, forms habits, acts, remembers, suffers pathology, and expresses will. Mental life cannot be reduced to bodily motion because sensations, relations, self-consciousness, value-feelings, and volitions are qualitatively and formally different from physical processes. Yet mind and body are lawfully and reciprocally connected.

The soul’s unity is the condition for appearance itself. Its cognitive life is active: it compares, relates, judges, attends, and gives form to experience. Its affective life is fundamental: feeling discloses value and grounds the sense that experiences are mine. Its volitional life consists not in magical muscular control but in the personal adoption of motives and decisions within a bodily order. Its logical life does not generate truth by psychological occurrence; validity remains distinct from mental genesis.

Metaphysically, finite souls belong to a larger order grounded in one Being or divine Good. Panpsychism is possible but not scientifically required. Immortality is not demonstrable from the concept of substance; if the soul endures, it is because of its value in the world-order, not because psychology proves it.

Historically, Lotze is a decisive bridge figure: after Kant, Herbart, Hegel, and German idealism; alongside physiology, psychophysics, and early scientific psychology; before Brentano, James, Frege, neo-Kantianism, phenomenology, pragmatism, and analytic philosophy. His philosophy of mind remains valuable precisely because it does not isolate consciousness from the body, science from value, selfhood from feeling, or psychology from metaphysics.

PreviousJohn Stuart Mill NextAlexander Bain

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
